stem: Find the flux of the constant vector field \( \mathbf{F} = ( [[a]], [[b]], [[c]] ) \) upward through the portion of the plane \( z = ([[alpha]])x + ([[beta]])y \) lying above the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \) (with upward-pointing normal).
solution: For \( z=g(x,y)=([[alpha]])x+([[beta]])y \) with upward normal, the flux is \( \iint_D (-P g_x - Q g_y + R)\,dA = \iint_D \big(([[c]]) - ([[a]])([[alpha]]) - ([[b]])([[beta]])\big)\,dA \). Over the rectangle this equals \( \big(([[c]]) - ([[a]])([[alpha]]) - ([[b]])([[beta]])\big)\cdot[[W]]\cdot[[H]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( +P g_x + Q g_y + R \), getting every sign in the normal wrong.
This forgets to multiply by the base area \( [[W]]\cdot[[H]] \).
This uses only the \( z \)-component \( R \), as if the surface were horizontal (ignoring the tilt).
This points the normal downward, flipping the sign of the flux.
This is off by one from the correct flux.
This is off by one from the correct flux.

stem: Find the flux of the vector field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = ( 0,\; 0,\; ([[a]])xy ) \) upward through the flat square \( S \) given by \( z=[[k]] \), \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \) (with upward-pointing normal).
solution: Since \( S \) is a flat horizontal plane with upward normal \( \mathbf{k} \), the flux reduces to \( \iint_D R(x,y,z)\,dA \) where \( R=([[a]])xy \) (the value of \( z=[[k]] \) does not enter, since \( R \) does not depend on \( z \)). So the flux is \( \int_0^{[[W]]}\int_0^{[[H]]} ([[a]])xy\,dy\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats \( R \) as if it were the CONSTANT \( a \) instead of the variable field \( axy \), skipping the double integration.
This mistakenly multiplies by the height \( k \) of the plane, which never enters (\( R \) does not depend on \( z \)).
This has the sign of the flux reversed.
This is off by one from the correct flux.
This is off by one from the correct flux.

stem: Find the flux of the constant vector field \( \mathbf{F} = ( [[a]], [[b]], [[c]] ) \) upward through the portion of the horizontal plane \( z = 0 \) lying above the triangle \( D \) with vertices \( (0,0) \), \( ([[W]],0) \), and \( (0, [[m]]\cdot[[W]]) \) (with upward-pointing normal \( \mathbf{k} \)). The triangle's hypotenuse is the line \( y = [[m]]([[W]]-x) \).
solution: Since \( S \) is a flat horizontal plane with upward normal \( \mathbf{k} \), the flux is \( \iint_D R\,dA = ([[c]])\cdot\text{Area}(D) \). The triangle has area \( \int_0^{[[W]]}([[m]])([[W]]-x)\,dx = \tfrac{1}{2}([[m]])([[W]])^{2} \), so the flux is \( ([[c]])\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}([[m]])([[W]])^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two unrelated horizontal components \( a,b \) to \( c \); only \( R=c \) contributes to flux through a horizontal plane.
This forgets the factor of \( \tfrac12 \) in the triangle's area.
This has the sign of the flux reversed.
This uses the bounding rectangle's dimensions directly instead of the triangle's actual area.
This is off by one from the correct flux.
This is off by one from the correct flux.
